activities_projects.title: Quantitative Validation of Mass Conservation
activities_projects.objective: To experimentally verify that the total mass of reactants equals the total mass of products in a chemical reaction[cite: 1439, 1466].
materials: Solutions of X and Y (e.g., Copper sulphate and Sodium carbonate) [cite: 1446]
steps: Prepare 5% solutions of the chosen pair of chemicals separately[cite: 1448].
Place solution Y in the flask and solution X in the ignition tube[cite: 1449].
Suspend the tube in the flask using a thread and seal with a cork to prevent mass loss to the atmosphere[cite: 1450, 1464].
Weigh the entire assembly accurately[cite: 1460].
Tilt and swirl the flask to mix the solutions and observe the chemical change[cite: 1461, 1463].
Reweigh the flask and compare the results[cite: 1462].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Demonstrates the Law of Conservation of Mass, proving mass is neither created nor destroyed during chemical transformations[cite: 1466].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why a cork is necessary for the accuracy of this experiment [cite: 1464] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Scale Modeling of Atomic Radii
activities_projects.objective: To visualize and compare the nanometric scale of atoms and molecules relative to macroscopic objects[cite: 1516, 1517].
steps: Review the unit conversion: $1/10^{9}	ext{m} = 1	ext{ nm}$[cite: 1519].
Draw a line representing 1 meter.
Mark the relative positions of an ant ($10^{-3}	ext{ m}$), a grain of sand ($10^{-4}	ext{ m}$), and an atom of hydrogen ($10^{-10}	ext{ m}$) on a logarithmic scale[cite: 1519].
Stack several sheets of paper and discuss how many millions of atoms would be required to reach that thickness[cite: 1518].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Develops a conceptual understanding of the insignificance of atomic size in the macroscopic world[cite: 1517, 1520].
activities_projects.evaluation: Convert the radius of a water molecule ($10^{-9}	ext{ m}$) into nanometers [cite: 1519] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Decoding Chemical Symbols and Origins
activities_projects.objective: To identify elements by their symbols and understand their etymological origins[cite: 1526, 1549].
steps: Identify symbols derived from English names (e.g., H for Hydrogen, Al for Aluminium)[cite: 1553, 1556, 1558].
Identify symbols derived from Latin, Greek, or German (e.g., Fe from ferrum, Na from natrium)[cite: 1561, 1562].
Practice writing symbols following IUPAC rules: first letter capital, second letter small[cite: 1552, 1554].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mastery of chemical shorthand and the standardized naming conventions of elements[cite: 1552, 1563].
activities_projects.evaluation: Write the Latin names and symbols for Iron, Sodium, and Potassium [cite: 1562] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Ratio by Mass in Compounds
activities_projects.objective: To verify the Law of Constant Proportions through mathematical analysis of compound compositions[cite: 1467, 1472].
steps: Analyze the mass ratio of Hydrogen to Oxygen in water ($1:8$)[cite: 1469].
Calculate the mass of oxygen required to react with a specific mass of hydrogen (e.g., 3 g)[cite: 1509].
Repeat the calculation for Ammonia ($NH_{3}$) using the ratio $14:3$[cite: 1471].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that elements are always present in definite proportions by mass in a chemical substance[cite: 1473].
activities_projects.evaluation: Determine the mass of oxygen needed to react with 3 g of hydrogen to form water [cite: 1509] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Valency-Based Formula Construction
activities_projects.objective: To use the 'criss-cross' method and combining capacity (valency) to write chemical formulae[cite: 1666, 1670, 1693].
materials: Table 3.6: Names and symbols of some ions [cite: 1679]
steps: Identify the valency or charge for given ions (e.g., $Mg^{2+}$, $Cl^{-}$)[cite: 1679].
Write the symbols of the cation first, followed by the anion[cite: 1702].
Criss-cross the valencies to determine the subscripts in the formula[cite: 1703].
Simplify ratios where necessary (e.g., $Ca_{2}O_{2}$ becomes $CaO$)[cite: 1744].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Ability to predict and write the symbolic representation of a compound's composition[cite: 1667].
activities_projects.evaluation: Write the chemical formulae for Aluminium oxide and Magnesium chloride [cite: 1716, 1705] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Molecules vs. Ions: Classification
activities_projects.objective: To differentiate between independent molecular units and charged ionic clusters[cite: 1602, 1656].
materials: List of substances: $Ar, O_{2}, NaCl, NaOH, (NH_{4})_{2}SO_{4}$ [cite: 1618, 1620, 1659, 1796]
steps: Classify substances as monoatomic, diatomic, or polyatomic[cite: 1618, 1620, 1622].
Identify 'anions' and 'cations' in ionic compounds[cite: 1658].
Recognize polyatomic ions (groups of atoms with a net charge)[cite: 1662].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between different ways atoms aggregate to form matter[cite: 1603].
activities_projects.evaluation: Define 'atomicity' and list the atomicity of Argon and Oxygen [cite: 1620, 1622] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Calculating Formula Unit and Molecular Mass
activities_projects.objective: To determine the relative mass of a substance by summing atomic masses[cite: 1789, 1813].
materials: Table 3.2: Atomic masses of a few elements [cite: 1600]
steps: Identify the atomic mass for each element in the compound[cite: 1600].
For molecular substances ($H_{2}O$), sum the atomic masses of all atoms in the molecule[cite: 1804, 1809].
For ionic substances ($CaCl_{2}$), calculate the formula unit mass using the same summation method[cite: 1814, 1825].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Applies the concept of relative atomic mass to find the total mass of molecules and ionic units[cite: 1790, 1818].
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the molecular mass of $HNO_{3}$ [cite: 1811] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Analyzing Postulates of Dalton's Theory
activities_projects.objective: To link Dalton's atomic theory postulates to established laws of chemical combination[cite: 1478, 1487].
steps: Read postulate (ii) regarding indivisible atoms and link it to the Law of Conservation of Mass[cite: 1494, 1510].
Read postulate (vi) regarding constant relative numbers of atoms and link it to the Law of Definite Proportions[cite: 1498, 1511].
Discuss how postulate (v) explains compound formation[cite: 1497].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the theoretical framework that provides the basis for chemical science laws[cite: 1484, 1486].
activities_projects.evaluation: Which postulate of Dalton's theory is a result of the Law of Conservation of Mass? [cite: 1510] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Polyatomic Ion Model Building
activities_projects.objective: To visualize clusters of atoms acting as single charged units[cite: 1662, 1842].
steps: Model a single ion (e.g., $Na^{+}$) as a single unit[cite: 1898].
Model a polyatomic ion (e.g., $SO_{4}^{2-}$) as a cluster that moves together[cite: 1899].
Demonstrate how two sodium units fix onto one sulphate unit to balance the charge[cite: 1901, 1902].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Clarifies the behavior of polyatomic ions in chemical combinations and formula writing[cite: 1688, 1846].
activities_projects.evaluation: Define polyatomic ions and give two examples [cite: 1857] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Predicting Ionic Compound Ratios
activities_projects.objective: To use mass ratios to find the simplest numerical ratio of atoms in a molecule[cite: 1631].
steps: Note the mass ratio for water (H:O = 1:8)[cite: 1628].
Divide mass ratios by atomic masses ($1/1$ and $8/16$)[cite: 1634, 1638].
Find the simplest whole-number ratio to derive the H:O = 2:1 atomic ratio[cite: 1654].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Bridges the gap between macroscopic mass measurements and microscopic atomic counts[cite: 1632, 1633].
activities_projects.evaluation: Show the calculation to find the atomic ratio for a water molecule [cite: 1633] (10 marks).
